content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 19, 2007


Mr. Joe Naiman
9312 Marilla Drive
Lakeside, CA 92040

RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-06-213

Dear Mr. Naiman:

     This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the campaign
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS

     1. May you use postage stamps for personal purposes that you originally
        purchased for use in your campaign for water district board member?

     2. If you have a committee in a future campaign, may extra postage stamps be
        used for personal purposes?

CONCLUSIONS

     1. Because you spent less than $1,000 on your campaign and you did not receive
        any contributions from others, it is permissible in this instance for you to use
        postage stamps that you have leftover from the campaign for your own
        purposes.

     2. Generally campaign funds or assets remaining after an election may not be
        used for personal purposes. Without the facts of your proposed candidacy, we
        are unable to answer this question specifically. However, the Analysis below
        may provide you with guidance.


¹ Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-06-213
Page No. 2

FACTS

You request information relating to the permissible use of leftover postage stamps
from your campaign. You spent less than $1,000 on your campaign for Riverview Water
District Board, and you self-financed your campaign in lieu of receiving contributions
from others. You were unsuccessful in your bid for the Riverview Water District Board.
You purchased first-class postage for campaign purposes but did not utilize all of the
stamps prior to the election. You would now like to use the stamps for personal
purposes.

ANALYSIS

Section 82013(a) defines a "committee" as:

". . . any person or combination of persons who directly or
indirectly . . . [r]eceives contributions totaling one thousand dollars
($1,000) or more in a calendar year . . .."

In addition, section 85201 states when a candidate is required to open a campaign
bank account:

"(a) Upon the filing of the statement of intention pursuant to Section
85200, the individual shall establish one campaign contribution account at
an office of a financial institution located in the state.
"(b) As required by subdivision (i) of Section 84102, a candidate who
raises contributions of one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more in a calendar
year shall set forth the name and address of the financial institution where
the candidate has established a campaign contribution account and the
account number on the committee statement of organization filed pursuant
to Sections 84101 and 84103 . . .."

* * *

"(g) This section does not apply to a candidate who will not receive
contributions and who makes expenditures from personal funds of less
than one thousand dollars ($1,000) in a calendar year to support his or her
candidacy. For purposes of this section, a candidate's payment for a filing
fee and statement of qualifications shall not be included in calculating the
total expenditures made.
"(h) An individual who raises contributions from others for his or her
campaign, but who raises or spends less than one thousand dollars
($1,000) in a calendar year, and does not qualify as a committee under
Section 82013, shall establish a campaign contribution account pursuant to
subdivision (a), but is not required to file a committee statement of
organization pursuant to Section 84101 or other statement of bank account
information."

File No. A-06-213
Page No. 3


If a candidate is required to open a campaign bank account, all contributions or
loans made to the candidate or the candidate's committee must be deposited in the
account. Any personal funds which will be used to promote the election of the candidate
must also be deposited into the account prior to expenditure. And all campaign
expenditures must be made from the account. (Sections 85201(c)-(e).)

You state that you spent less than $1,000 on your campaign for water district
board. You also told us that all the money you did spend on your campaign was from
your own funds; you did not receive any contributions from others. Under the standards
set forth above, therefore, you did not have a committee as defined by section
82013(a), and you were not required to establish a campaign bank account under section
85201(g). The only campaign filing obligation a candidate in your situation would have had is to
file the candidate campaign statement short form (Form 470) declaring that you raised
and spent less than $1,000 during the calendar year.

To ensure the integrity of the use of funds raised by a candidate to run for elective
office, the Act contains restrictions on the use of campaign funds raised by a candidate to
run for elective office. Under these sections, campaign funds are held in trust for expenses related to campaign
purposes and the personal use of campaign funds is not permitted. Section 89510(b)
provides:

"All contributions deposited into the campaign account shall be deemed to
be held in trust for expenses associated with the election of the candidate
or for expenses associated with holding office." (Emphasis added.)

Section 89511 describes the applicability of the restrictions on the personal use of
campaign funds:

"(a) This article applies to campaign funds held by candidates for elective
office, elected officers, controlled committees, ballot measure committees,
committees opposed to a candidate or measure, and any committee which
qualifies as a committee pursuant to subdivision (a) of Section 82013."
"(b)(1) For purposes of this chapter, 'campaign funds' includes any
contributions, cash, cash equivalents, and other assets received or possessed by
a committee as defined by subdivision (a) of Section 82013 . . .."
(Emphasis added.)

Because you spent less than $1,000 in personal funds on your campaign and did
not any raise contributions from others, you did not have a committee under section
82013(a) and were not required to establish a campaign bank account under section
89501(g). Therefore, the restrictions on the use of campaign funds set forth in 89510(b)
and chapter 9.5, article 4, are not applicable in this instance. Consequently, it is

File No. A-06-213
Page No. 4

permissible for you to use the extra postage stamps you originally purchased for your
campaign for your own purposes.

With regard to your question about a future election, if you spend $1,000 or more
of personal funds or if you receive contributions from others, you will be required to open
a campaign bank account under section 85201, discussed above. If you are required to
have a bank account under section 85201, and you wish to use personal funds to support
your candidacy, you must place personal funds into the campaign contribution account
prior to expending them. For example, if you decide to use personal funds to purchase
postage stamps for use by your campaign, you will be required to first place the funds
into the campaign contribution account and then make the purchase from the account.
The funds must be reported on campaign disclosure reports as a contribution from you,
either as a monetary contribution on Schedule A of the Form 460 (Recipient Committee
Campaign Statement) or as a loan on Schedule B. (Section 84211.) Following the
campaign or your term of office, if your candidacy is successful, leftover campaign funds
or assets would be subject to the surplus funds provisions of the Act. (Section 89519).
The Form 460 and other informational materials are available on the Commission's
website (www.fppc.ca.gov).

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Kevin S. Moen, PhD
Political Reform Consultant II
